coredata.dc:description: Lignin, a sustainable and inexpensive material is becoming a popular candidate in research because of its potential to be converted into value-added products. Due to the fluorescence and biocompatibility, lignin-based carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) are regarded as a promising option for applications like drug delivery, diagnostics, imaging, and photodynamic treatment. This review tries to give an outline of the fluorescence properties of LCQDs and their origin. Different proposals that are used to explain the fluorescence ability of LCQDs are discussed in a comprehensive way focusing on the advantages and disadvantages. Applications of LCQDs in the biomedical field, their properties, and the limiting factors to the development of commercial biomedical products are discussed. This review tries to motivate researchers in this field to develop new approaches and techniques for the utilization of LCQDs in a highly sophisticated way.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Lignin, a sustainable and inexpensive material is becoming a popular candidate in research because of its potential to be converted into value-added products. Due to the fluorescence and biocompatibility, lignin-based carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) are regarded as a promising option for applications like drug delivery, diagnostics, imaging, and photodynamic treatment. This review tries to give an outline of the fluorescence properties of LCQDs and their origin. Different proposals that are used to explain the fluorescence ability of LCQDs are discussed in a comprehensive way focusing on the advantages and disadvantages. Applications of LCQDs in the biomedical field, their properties, and the limiting factors to the development of commercial biomedical products are discussed. This review tries to motivate researchers in this field to develop new approaches and techniques for the utilization of LCQDs in a highly sophisticated way.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The development of functional materials from a natural resource by following green and sustainable methodologies is considered as an alternative for petroleum-based materials and is considered as a better choice for a sustainable world ahead (Hammitt et al., 1992; Zhou et al., 2007). Due to their potential to biodegrade, synthetic polymers like poly(-caprolactone), poly(lactide), and poly(3-hydroxybutyrate) (PHB) as well as natural polymers like lignin, cellulose, and chitin are receiving more and more attention (Miller, 2013; Zhu et al., 2016). Among these highly complex and irregular polymers, lignin has demonstrated its effectiveness as a photo luminescent material and is consequently used for a variety of applications in diverse domains, including cell-wall imaging to fluorescent sensor (Beaucamp et al., 2019; Chabbert et al., 2018; Donaldson and Radotic, 2013; Ji et al., 2013; Qian et al., 2018).

Cell-wall fluorescence is associated with the UV or visible light excitation of the natural biopolymer lignin (Coletta et al., 2013; Ma et al., 2018; Xue et al., 2016a; Yu et al., 2021). To stop industrial waste products containing lignin from contaminating the environment it is advised to develop value-added goods using these resources(Shen et al., 2022)(Yu et al., 2021). As a result, research is intensifying on the development of functional materials based on lignin through the chemical or thermal processing of waste materials (Maceda and Terrazas, 2022; Paul et al., 2022; Yuan et al., 2011; Lingli Zhu et al., 2022b). Blue-green fluorescence can be produced by lignin when it is excited by UV or visible light(Zhang et al., 2023b). Thus, the natural polymer lignin's capacity for fluorescence in the 350–650 nm regime has made it useful in cutting-edge applications (Chen et al., 2019). Lignin fluorescence is now used for a variety of purposes, including cell-wall imaging, advanced functional sensing, and medical applications. In light of this, lignin's capacity to fluoresce offers new information about its use and application (Maceda and Terrazas, 2022; Paul et al., 2022; Lingli Zhu et al., 2022b).

Lignin-based quantum dots are a new class of materials with a wide range of applications in materials research. These have been used to detect a wide range of ions and chemical compounds. These materials exhibit fluorescence, engaging them in advanced applications. One of the major advantage of LCQDs involves the tailorable broadband PL, which ensures fast photo response in visible range and long term stability (Wang et al., 2023). LCQDs will also ensures economic and ecological advantages over conventional QDs. LCQDs are significant in the environmental monitoring and cell imaging (Wei et al., 2022). One of the major advantages of synthesis of CQDs from lignin lies in the yield, in one study it is reported that it is possible to synthesis LCQDs with a yield of 82% higher than the weight of the alkali lignin, the precursor(Zhang et al., 2019). Enlarged conjugation and higher yield are the major advantageous of LCQDs compared to conventional QDs(Gao et al., 2023). LCQDs synthesized from lignin is having comparable properties and have less particle size compared to the conventional QDs( Table 1). Hence lignin can be considered as an alternative choice for the synthesis of QDs.

By using various chemical modification processes, such as grafting (Hong et al., 2016; Moreno and Sipponen, 2020; Panovic et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2020), amine functionalization (Heo et al., 2022), azo functionalization (Pandian et al., 2020) etc., fluorescent materials based on lignin can be created. Apart from the functionalized lignin, lignin derived nanoparticles are showing excellent fluorescence ability (Gu et al., 2020; Ma et al., 2018; X. Wang et al., 2021b; Zhou et al., 2020). Among the various nanoparticles, lignin-derived carbon dots, such as fluorescent lignin graphene quantum dots (LGQDs) and fluorescent lignin carbon quantum dots (LCQDs), are exhibiting outstanding fluorescence properties and are considered as a new class of materials (JANUS et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019). Due to quantum confinement, quantum dots, which have all three dimensions in the nano regime, exhibit exceptional features. They are regarded as functional prospects for sensing, optronics, and bio-imaging applications because of their inherent fluorescence features (Alex et al., 2020; Jayan et al., 2021).

This review discusses, recent advancements and future prospects of lignin-based fluorescent carbon dots emphasizing on their in biomedical applications. Additionally, this review aims to shed information on the cause of the fluorescence of lignin macromolecules and LCQDs. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first review on the biomedical applications of fluorescent quantum dots based on lignin.

Fluorescence spectroscopy is regarded to be an effective instrument for analysing wood fibers, paper, and determining the components of lignin. Because of the extraordinary fluorescence properties of lignin, the presence of strongly aromatic components in lignin was investigated. However, the cause of the lignin fluorescence is still unknown. According to numerous researches, the existence of diverse structures such as stilbene structures, coniferyl alcohol anion structures, and phenyl coumarone structures explains the fluorescence (Albinsson et al., 1999; Xue et al., 2016b). However, these structures are unable to properly and adequately describe the mechanism of fluorescence. Phenyl coumarone compounds, which are produced through a variety of processes, aid in acting as a potential acceptor. These structures serve as "energy sinks," and light is released from them. Fig. 1a shows the structure of lignin. The lignin particle aggregation-induced emission behaviour is depicted in Fig. 1b and c. It is evident that when the ethanol concentration rises, the lignin particle aggregation in the poor solvent increases as a result of both intramolecular and intermolecular aggregation.

Due to the presence of ether linkages and non-conjugated carbon-carbon bonds, lignin is well attached to various phenyl propane units (Rinaldi et al., 2016). (Rinaldi et al., 2016). Finding the true cause of the lignin fluorescence is exceedingly difficult because low conjugation is in conflict with the capacity to fluoresce, particularly the long wavelength emission (Albinsson et al., 1999; Djikanović et al., 2007; Tylli et al., 1995). It was challenging to identify conjugated structures in the lignin molecule (Auxenfans et al., 2017; Donaldson, 2013; Lähdetie et al., 2013). In-situ plant cell treatment and model compound methods (Djikanović et al., 2007; Tylli et al., 1995) were utilized for this identification process (Auxenfans et al., 2017; Lähdetie et al., 2013). In the model compound, the fluorescence activity of lignin is compared with the fluorescence of other known materials, and it is observed that the fluorescence corresponding to conjugated compounds like biphenyl, phenyl coumarin, anion of coniferyl alcohol and stilbene was shown by lignin (Lanzalunga and Bietti, 2000; R. Liu et al., 2020a); but it does not coincide completely with the lignin fluorescence. Dehydrogenative polymer (DHP) was also utilized in the modelling, but even the structure of DHP was simpler than lignin, the problem solving was very complicated (Radotić et al., 2006; Thomson et al., 2007). Hence the fluorescence activity remained mysterious after the modelling study (Donaldson et al., 2010). But later it was observed that lignin is a multi-fluorophore system, fluorescence spectra is due to the merging of multiple fluorophores. But the fitting models failed to explain the multi-fluorophore behaviour of lignin, hence the reason behind the fluorescence remained concealed (Luo et al., 2001; Patzke et al., 2002; Xue et al., 2020). Later the luminophore behaviour was proposed, which suggests that the fluorescence of lignin is like as it contains only one luminophore in it (Imai, 2021; Takada et al., 2022). It is further explained by the energy cassette concept, where energy transfer occurs between the fluorophores, leading to the formation of a coupling state by altering its original energy state (Cadena-Caicedo et al., 2019; Kern et al., 2014; Miyawaki, 2011; Xue et al., 2020) So, researchers were incapable of explaining the fluorescence ability of lignin micelle. In in-situ method, the plant cells were studied before and after the direct treatment and it was understood that even after immobilization of lignin it was responsive to chemical as well physical treatments because of the presence of conjugation. Moreover, it was showing red shift under pressure due to higher conjugation degree (Heise et al., 2021; Kai et al., 2016; Radotić et al., 2006). Later, it was observed that if the 3D structure of lignin is distorted it will lead to the reduction in fluorescence intensity. Thus, apart from the chemical structure, the microstructure of lignin plays a vital role in its fluorescence behaviour as a result of the energy transfer. Thus, the luminophore in the lignin micelle is actually the coupling state of fluorophores in it (Auxenfans et al., 2017).

Recently, it is observed that the natural amphiphilic lignin polymer is capable of forming aggregates and the aggregated induced conjugation is responsible for the fluorescence rather than traditional conjugated luminescence (Ma et al., 2018). Xue et al. (Xue et al., 2016c). reported the aggregation induced emission of lignin for the first time which is responsible for the fluorescence of lignin. In subsequent study, Xue et al.(Xue et al., 2020) studied the fluorescence behaviour using 3D fluorescence spectroscopy and the study revealed that lignin fluorescence is not only distributed in long-wavelength region but also in short-wavelength region as shown in Fig. 2b. Moreover, the maximum excitation spectrum is overlapped with the UV spectra as well, thus the behaviour cannot be explained only by the conventional theory and thus they put forward the aggregation induced conjugation effect. They confirmed that in addition to the aggregation fluorescence behaviour; other factors like physical configuration, chemical structure, presence of main fluorophore arrived from the aggregation of phenylpropane unit in it are affecting the fluorescence. Multiple fluorophores will aggregate in nano micelle of lignin and helps in the development of coupling state and the mechanism as shown in Fig. 1f.

Among the various models that are utilized so far to explain the fluorescence, the aggregation induced conjugation effect is more recent and is getting wider attention. But researchers are still trying to elucidate the mechanism of fluorescence of lignin and in near future we can expect development of new models. But more details are required to confirm the exact fluorescence mechanism, it is needed to confirm whether fluorophore, luminophore or aggregation induced conjugation or combination of all is behind the fluorescence. Moreover, clarity is required in the source and fluorescence behaviour, as the resource changes the fluorescence behaviour varies. Hence it has to be confirmed that many other factors in the biomass are responsible for the fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Origin of fluorescence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: In quantum dots, even though their optical characteristics have garnered a lot of interest, the origin of tunable PL continues as a hot topic of discussion. Because of the nanoscale dimensions and the nonzero band gap, the associated PL characteristics of the quantum dots are anticipated to be swiftly adjusted by altering the surface chemistry and carbon core. In order to explain the origin of luminescence in CQDs, two accepted mechanistic models have been proposed. They are (a) the conjugated π-domains-based band gap emission, where the quantum confinement effect holds significance; (b) surface defect states, which are predominantly apparent as surface effects(Shi et al., 2022).

CQDs often exhibit high optical absorption in the ultraviolet range, with a tail that extends into the visible range. The π–π* transition of aromatic C–C bonds is accountable for the maximum peak observed at 230 nm in the carbon core, whereas the n–π* transition of CO bonds or other connected groups is accountable for the shoulder observed at 300 nm(Y. Wang et al., 2014b). CQD fluorescence emission is a quite complicated phenomenon that is still not well understood. Traditionally, CQDs were studied at fixed excitation wavelength which was pH-dependent PL. A new photoluminescence process was postulated by Dagar et al., observed under different pH for a wide range of excitation(Dager et al., 2019). This was multicolor imaging using excitation-dependent photoluminescence behaviour. According to Wang et al., the emission peak is always related to the stimulation wavelength. We refer to this phenomenon as wavelength-dependent behaviour. This may be the consequence of widely dispersed variations in CQD sizes, surface chemistry, varying emissive wavelengths, or mechanism still to be proven(Dager et al., 2019).

PL can be measured using various data analysis methods, principal component analysis, partial least square analysis and multivariate curve resolution (Felten et al., 2019). Recent years have witnessed a major increase in interest in up-conversion luminescence of CQDs, an optical phenomenon that results from anti-Stokes luminescence that generates high-energy photons by absorbing two or more low-energy photons.

In comparison to the electron's de Broglie wavelength within the dot, the particle size of the carbon quantum dots is small, hence a deviation from the bulk properties is created, thus leading to a quantum confinement effect. Because of this effect, energy is quantized into discrete levels in the conduction and valence bands, allowing the dot to be implicated as a "virtual atom." The particle's radius determines the emission energy, causing the excitation and emission spectra to shift to shorter wavelengths as the particle get smaller. The quantum confinement effect is affected by changes in parameters, synthesis processes, and stroke shift. It was also observed that by doping few elements like boron or nitrogen or sulfur or phosphorus, it is possible to tune CQDs emission spectra (Vibhute et al., 2022). One of the most effective ways to fine tune the optical characteristics of the quantum dots is heteroatom doping. These doped quantum dots have gained much focus because of their easy synthesis routes and performance. Research has demonstrated enhancement in fluorescence and shift in emission spectra due to doping. Incorporation of doped atoms in the quantum dots create traps and different emission centres in them. This results in the tuning of the emission spectra, i.e., to blue and even red regions(Vibhute et al., 2022). A schematic of the characteristics, applications and synthesis process of CDs are presented in Fig. 2.

Zhang et al.(Zhang et al., 2019) synthesized of bright green fluorescence nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots from alkali lignin by a simple one-step route. These CQDs display excellent photoluminescence properties and are employed as fluorescent probes in metal ion detection and cell imaging. Using a green bottom-up approach, Wang et al.(Wang et al., 2019) synthesized graphene quantum dots based on lignin. The ultra sensitivity of LCQDs for H2O2 detection is investigated through density functional theory (DFT) calculations. The findings indicate that the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) behaviour(Wei et al., 2022), which is first time proposed and then confirmed by a number of control experiments, dominates the fluorescence mechanism of LCQDs. This work provides a sustainable route for the synthesis of S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots as ultrasensitive biosensors. Alongside, the theoretical findings provide a thorough comprehension of the photoluminescence phenomena of LCQDs.

It also should be pointed that due to the diversity in the structure of the precursor, the mechanism behind the fluorescence of LCQDs is a debating topic(L. Chen et al., 2017b; L. Wang et al., 2014a). Wang et al.(Wang et al., 2019) confirmed that the fluorescence mechanism in LCQDs is dominant by the intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) properties. the edge groups and the heteroatoms present in LCQDs contribute to the HOMO and LUMO. Thus, the intrinsic structure plays a crucial role in tuning the fluorescence of LCQDs. In case LCQDS-SO2, hence the photo-excited electrons (PEEs) will get decayed to HOMO and shows intensity changes. Hence the S groups in the edges will help in the detection of H2O2. Based on the conjugated theory, the presence of highly conjugated benzene structures is responsible for the fluorescence mechanism of LCQDs. So that the functionalization of LCQDs with highly conjugated functional groups can enhance the fluorescence properties of LCQDs(Zhao et al., 2021). In another study, it is revealed that the presence of internal conjugated SP2 in the carbon core and the amorphous region present over the surfaces are the main cause for multicoloured fluorescence mechanism. So, with increase in graphitization, these materials will show a red shift in the PL emission(Zhu et al., 2021b)(Zheng et al., 2021).

It is also reported that the functionalization of LCQDs can be utilized as a method for tuning the fluorescence properties(Zhang et al., 2023a) under the same irradiation light as shown in Fig. 3a. Moreover, the dopants can also be used for tuning the properties like broad spectral emissions and the tuning can be attained by controlling the doping conditions and the type of dopant used (Fig. 3b). The rich electron groups will try to occupy the energy levels of LCQDs and lift the fermi levels (Fig. 3b). the molecular fluorescence theory differs from the surface state theory. The theory specifies that the formation of fluorophores is responsible for the fluorescence properties, hence the effect of surface functionalization or chemical modification on tuning the fluorescence can be explained (Fig. 3e)(Gan et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent lignin carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Generally top-down and bottom-up approaches are followed for the fabrication of carbon quantum dots (CQDs). Top-down commonly refers to the strategies which are adopted for the development from bulk materials (Choi et al., 2018; Crista et al., 2020; Sendão et al., 2020; Yang et al., 2013) like graphene oxide (Dong et al., 2012; Li et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2011), carbon nanotubes (Bak et al., 2016; Chung et al., 2021; Shinde and Pillai, 2012), graphite (Kwon et al., 2014; Sun et al., 2013) either by chemical (Crista et al., 2020; Xiong et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2013), mechanical or physical means. Compared to these carbonaceous materials, carbon rich biowaste materials are utilized for the synthesis of CQDs (Ahuja et al., 2022; Boruah et al., 2020; Devi et al., 2017). In bottom-up approach, CQDs are formed by the aggregation of small organic molecules by the methods (Bansal et al., 2015; Rigodanza et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020) like carbonization (Liu et al., 2017; Prasad et al., 2019), pyrolysis (Beri et al., 2011; Salaramoli et al., 2013) or by gradual chemical synthesis (Hu et al., 2021; Jing et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2012). In this method, organic molecules are generally utilized, but expense and environmental threads are the serious concern (Liu et al., 2019; Zheng et al., 2013). The production of CQDs from bio-waste is considered as a better way for the production of CQDs(Yang et al., 2021), moreover it is a solution for environmental crisis (Pramanik et al., 2018; Rajamanikandan et al., 2021; Rodríguez-Padrón et al., 2020). Hence the production of CQDs from lignin is the prudent choice as lignin is considered as the major waste from rice mill, paper, pulp industries called black liquor (Zhao et al., 2021)(Yi et al., 2017; Zhang et al., 2017). Microwave assisted treatment, hydrothermal treatment, pyrolysis, chemical treatments, carbonization etc. (Paul et al., 2022; Zhu et al., 2021b) are the general methods followed for the production of different types CQDs, like LGQDs and LCQDs from lignin. The major methods used for the production CQDs from lignin and its properties are tabulated in Table 2.

Yang et al.(Yang et al., 2020) developed a simple method for converting lignin-based waste to fluorescent CQDs by hydrothermal treatment in sulfuric aqueous solution, which was then utilized to detect Sudan I in an acidic environment. Kavgacı et al.(Kavgaci et al., n.d.) synthesized fluorescent CQDs with hydrothermal and solvothermal method and used them for anti-counterfeiting and encryption. Zhao et al.(Zhao et al., 2023) synthesized N, Cl co-doped lignin CQDs and detected microplastics in water. Zhu et al.(Zhu et al., 2021b) used a green synthesis route for preparing tuneable fluorescent CQDs from lignin and its application in anti-counterfeit printing CQDs were employed by Tuccitto et al.(Tuccitto et al., 2021) as fluorescent nano chemosensors in their effort to selectively detect amino acids. Yang et al.(Yang et al., 2021) developed magnesium- and nitrogen-co-doped CQDs with bright green fluorescence from lignin.

Lignin based CQDs are getting wide attention due to their size related tunable properties, especially the fluorescence and due to its sustainable nature. From the different methods used for the preparation of LCQDs, it is noteworthy that hydrothermal methods are highly effective for the preparation due to the size as well as the yield. For reasons of sustainability, hydrothermal procedures are favoured over chemical treatments or modifications. Moreover, the size of LCQDs made by following hydrothermal method are less than 5 nm, it can also be utilized for the synthesis of LCQDs from alkali lignin and even from the barks of trees and fruits. Hence irrespective of the resource, the hydrothermal method can be considered as an effective approach in synthesizing carbon dots. The combination of these treatments is also found effective in the fabrication of LCQDs, the selection of a suitable method depends on the nature of resource, desired particle size, expected properties etc. Hydrothermal treatments are considered as best for the synthesis of LCQDs from biomass because of excellent fluorescence properties and size characteristics (less than 5 nm). From the different methods, it can be understood hydrothermal method can be considered as an effective method for the synthesis of very small LCQDs and it can be utilized for any lignin source. Microwave assisted synthesis method is very easy, cheap, effective and less time-consuming than other methods. This method helps in the synthesis of LCQDs with size less than 5 nm. The hydrothermal followed mechanical synthesis is complex due to the two step pathway. But the fractionation or acid treatment followed hydrothermal method can be considered as an effective way, in which break down of lignin in the first step ensures the formation of small sized LCQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Significance of lignin in biomedical field
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the high demand of sustainable development, there is high interest in the development of lignin-based value-added products (Saito et al., 2012; Sivagurunathan et al., 2021). Natural polymers like cellulose, alginate, chitin, chitosan etc. are having potential applications in biomedical field like drug delivery, tissue engineering (Anitha et al., 2014; Puscaselu et al., 2020; Seddiqi et al., 2021). Compared to other biopolymers, lignin is rarely employed for biomedical application owing to its origin from multiple resources and extraction process. The unawareness on the breakdown of lignin in living body is a serious concern, this restricts its utility in biomedical applications, even though it is biocompatible and biodegradable in nature (Erdocia et al., 2021; Figueiredo et al., 2018; Moreno and Sipponen, 2020). Despite of the drawbacks, it possess other properties like antioxidant and anti-UV properties, as well as good antimicrobial activities which are not usually found in other natural polymers (Chee et al., 2022; Espinoza-Acosta et al., 2016; Su et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022). Due to these advantages, recently (over 5 years) its utilization is improved much as reported by Sugarito et al.(Sugiarto et al., 2022) as shown in Fig. 4a-b. Different forms of lignin like lignin nanoparticles, hydrogels etc. are being utilized in the biomedical field (R. Liu et al., 2020a). Thus, coming to the nano level, lignin is capable of showing advanced applications owing to its outstanding properties attributed by the size effects. Moreover, lignin nanoparticles are potentially used in drug delivery, bio imaging and gene delivery(Figueiredo et al., 2018; R. Liu et al., 2020a). As the research on lignin-based materials for biomedical applications are continuously improving, the practical use is expected in the near future. The era of lignin-based materials in biomedical is in its emerging phase. Disregarding other reviews on lignin-based materials in biomedical application, this review focus on the development of lignin based CQDs in biomedical fields.

The biocompatibility is the key factor for the use of lignin and its various fluorescence forms in cellular imaging(Z. Liu et al., 2020b) Moreover, the use of lignin and lignin-based carbon dots for biological application is considered as a simple, sustainable, efficient, selective and sensitive way (Niu et al., 2017; Pathak et al., 2017). Extra ordinary superiority, environmental friendliness, low cytotoxicity etc. make lignin a promising candidate(Yu et al., 2021). Due to the chemical structure, pi-pi aggregation induced molecular aggregation and aggregation induced fluorescence are responsible for the fluorescence effect of lignin (Deng et al., 2011). As lignin is capable of forming molecular aggregates, it can be considered as a suitable material for the formation of fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for biological application. Thus, carbon dots prepared from alkali or any kind of lignin will show fluorescence. Hence fluorescent lignin carbon quantum dots (LCQDs) are developed from lignin for advanced applications especially for fluorescence imaging. Shi et al.(Shi et al., 2019) the synthesis of CQDs with high fluorescence ability via modifying the bio-waste through treatments like carbonization and ball milling. The cell viability of LCQDs were tested in mouse macrophage cell lines and it was seen that even after the addition of 100 µg/mL, the cell viability remained 97%. Cytocompatibility and bio-imaging capability were analysed by introducing LCQDs into macrophage cells and analysed using laser scanning confocal microscope. LCQDs were capable of penetrating into the cell membrane and reaching the cytoplasm but not the nuclear membrane. Thus, the LCQDs can be considered as promising candidates for bio-imaging and bio-sensing applications. In order to avoid the defect of molecular aggregation, suitable methods should be adopted for the fabrication of LCQDs for maintaining the fluorescence. Hence harsh treatments should be avoided for obtaining better fluorescent ability. Niu et al.(Niu et al., 2017) thus adopted organic extraction, centrifugation followed dialysis for the synthesis of CQDs from lignin; centrifugation ensures the aggregation and shows excitation at 320 nm and maximum emission at 400 nm. In vitro cell studies using 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) assays confirmed bio-compatibility of 90% even at higher loadings of 800 µg/L. Green-one and blue-green two photon fluorescence images of Hela cells in the inverted fluorescence microscopy confirms the cellular up taking ability of LCQDs (Fig. 4c-h). By incorporating the luminescent molecule DAPI, the imaging effect of LCQDs were further confirmed. The low cytotoxicity, useful imaging effect and better cell permeability makes LCQDs a promising bioimaging material. Later, it is observed that the LCQDs are having lamellar-core shell like multi-layered graphene structure with active oxygen bearing functionalities around. The fluorescence intensity is greatly affected by the concentration as well as dopants present in it. Amine functionalization of LCQDs ensures higher fluorescence, moreover the LCQDs cultivated cells shows brighter fluorescence and thus are highly advanced for biomedical applications. Lignin based Graphene Quantum Dots (LGQDs) developed by green fractionation technologies are structures in nanoscopic regime with a few layered structures are having a spectrum of applications due to its water soluble, bright fluorescence, biocompatibility and long term photostability. They can be used for the fabrication of probes for the detection of hydrogen peroxide which are fluorescent- switchable in nature (Wang et al., 2019). Lignin derived fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) were capable of showing cellular uptake as well as cell viability because of inherent biocompatibility, they can be utilized for in vitro as well as in-vivo imaging. The bio-imaging study carried out in cancerous cells like human lung adenocarcinoma and colon cancer cell line confirmed the diffusion ability of FCDs into the cytoplasmic as well as the nucleus of the cells and helped in lightening the whole cell, but it was possible to distinguish both the nucleus and cytoplasm. Moreover, the MTT assay test and hemocompatibility test confirmed the biocompatibility of FCDs i.e it was capable of showing lower cytotoxicity and blood compatibility. But owing to the biological origin of lignin(i.e the unawareness about the molecular breakdown in living body), the cytotoxicity at higher concentration is still a challenge (Rai et al., 2017).

Carbonization followed chemical oxidation was carried out by Myint et al. (Myint et al., 2018) for the production of lignin based FCDs with an excitation wavelength independent emission characteristic as shown in Fig. 4 i-j. The prepared FCDs showed better biocompatibility, the Hela cells (Breast Cancer Cells) were viable at 500 mg mL−1 even after 48 h incubation confirming the lower cytotoxicity characteristics. The ability of FCDS in imaging Hela cells confirmed the application of lignin FCDs as fluorescence probe.

Before cellular imaging as well as clinical usage, the cytotoxicity has to be checked. In a study carried out by Gao et al. (Gao et al., 2019) the MTT assay test carried out in Hela cells confirmed that the LCQDs does not alter the cell viability as shown in Fig. 5. Even though the concentration of LCQDs reached 1 mg/mL, the cell viability was 90%. Thus, LCQDs were used for the imaging of Hela cells and in the presence of Fe3+ ions, the intensity of spectral lines reduced confirming the detection action of LCQDs towards ferric ion. For potential brain imaging applications Pei et al. (Pei et al., 2021) showed that the nitrogen doped LCQDs were capable of crossing the brain barrier which is an advantage of its small size. With the rise in concentration, the cell uptake got improved with a maximum at a concentration of 12.5%, after that the cell viability showed a negative impact. From their study, it was observed that the nitrogen doped LCQDS showed a cytotoxicity after a particular concentration. Hence compared to LCQDs, N-doped LCQDs have negative impact on cell viability and cause cytotoxicity.

Recently, applied nano biotechnology researchers have started utilizing the CQDS developed from lignin for the fabrication of fluorescence probes for a cellular microscopic study. Atchudan et al. (Atchudan et al., 2022) utilized the fluorescence action of LCQDs for the imaging of human colon cancer because of higher cell viability of about 97% with 200 mg/mL concentration of LCQDs. Healthy status was confirmed even after incubation of 24 h. The colon cells did not show any fluorescence in the absence of LCQDs, but in presence LCQDs it showed bright multicolour emission. Moreover, in presence of Fe3+ ions the quenching effect was also observed. Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence images.

CDs are considered as best candidates for drug delivery applications due to its biocompatibility and loading efficiency (Biswas et al., 2021; Matea et al., 2017). Hence nanocarriers made from CDs nanogels by combining chitosan and CDs are found very effective in tumour treatment with effective drug releasing effect (Wang et al., 2017). The drug loading efficiency of LCQDs can be demonstrated by taking example of the study carried out by Rai et al.(Rai et al., 2017);they have loaded curcumin in LCQDs and analysed its dug loading efficiency and drug release efficiency. Curcumin got attached with LCQDs by hydrogen bonding interaction and hydrophobic(major) interaction in presence of the acetic acid. They also observed that the pH value plays a vital role in the release of curcumin from LCQDs, maximum release is observed at pH of 9.1, due to the hydrophilicity of curcumin and increased solubility. They proposed the reason behind the release of curcumin is due to the decrease in hydrophobic interaction between the curcumin and LCQDs. Thus, the pH responsive drug release efficiency is highly useful in finding applications in drug release in blood and cells and can be utilized in future for drugs other than curcumin.

It is observed that the CQDs developed from beer were capable of showing drug loading efficiency with Doxorubicin (DOX). DOX were successfully incorporated with CDs because of the electrostatic interaction. DOX-CQDS were utilized as a functional probe for the bio-imaging and the cytotoxicity studies were carried out in human breast cancer using MTT assay. Compared to the free DOX, DOX-CQDS showed lesser cytotoxicity due to the controlled release of DOX. The controlled release of drugs is a highly positive requirement for the utilization as a drug carrier. Hence CQDS can be considered as a potential drug carrier (Wang et al., 2015). Moreover, it is possible to trigger the drug loading efficiency of fluorescent CQDs, by tethering a photo trigger onto it. The photo regulated drug releasing efficiency is characterized by adjusting the light, but certain photo triggers like Qucbl are hazardous to human cells. Hence the study of photo responsive characteristics of LCQDs can revolutionize the biomedical field. The self-triggered drug loading efficiency of LCQDs are thus considered as a hot topic in this arena. Though CQDs are utilized as drug carriers in several reports, very limited studies have focused on the drug loading and releasing efficiency of fluorescent LCQDs (Karthik et al., 2013). Depending on the characteristics of functionalities present over the CQDs, it can be utilized for the gene and drug delivery applications (J. Chen et al., 2017a; Dou et al., 2015; Pantarotto et al., 2004; Singh et al., 2005). Cationic CQDs will have higher interaction towards the negatively charged nucleic acids and are very suitable for gene delivery.

For cancer theranostics generally positively charged carriers are utilized, hence positively charged CQDs can be employed for cancer treatment as it gets attracted with the cancer cell membranes with negative charge (B. Chen et al., 2016a). Hence these CQDs can be conjugated with anticancer drugs like DDX, oxaliplatin and cisplatin for treating cancer cells (Feng et al., 2016; Gong et al., 2016; Li et al., 2016). Due to the inherent anti-tumour ability of CQDs, besides the drug, the carrier itself can show action against cancer cells (Zheng et al., 2016). CQDs are anti-carcinogenic as well as antibacterial in nature. Hence, they are considered as a promising candidate for medical applications (Li et al., 2018). John et al. (John et al., 2020) prepared fluorescent CQDs from rice bran and are effectively utilized for drug loading applications. The drug delivery applications were evaluated by conjugating CQDS with amoxycillin (AMX), CQDS showed retention of fluorescence even after the conjugation. The conjugated system showed higher drug loading capacity compared to other amoxycillin carriers (CQD: AMX: 2:800) and with increasing concentration of AMX, the CQDs showed improved conjugation. In order to identify the stability of the drug release rate, the release rate was analysed in PBS solution at different pH. the CQDs-AMX showed rapid and stable release and after a day the release rate become constant for all the loadings. The system showed maximum drug release at acidic medium due to the protonation of AMX β-lactam ring. Thus, CQDs are capable of showing a pH sensitive drug release which can be utilized for specific targeted drug release in future. CQDs are highly active as therapeutic agent, and can be considered as a raw material for gene and drug delivery applications. Even though there are studies on the drug delivery applications of CQDs (Wan et al., 2021), very limited studies are reported on the therapeutic application of lignin based CQDs. There is certain concern to biomass derived CQDs in drug delivery applications. One has to confirm when preparing CQDs from different resources, whether the characteristics of resources get maintained in CQDs or not. Whether it is exactly the effect of CQDs or the resource have any effect in the drug loading characteristics. Still lot of assumptions are there in the fluorescence of lignin and its CQDs hence a detailed analysis is required to confirm the lignin based CQDs as a promising therapeutic agent. The effect of synthesis mechanism, structure etc. have to be clearly studied before the application of CQDs into body. Hence very limited literatures are dealing with the therapeutic application of lignin based CQDs. Due to the active fluorescence ability, less cytotoxicity CDs from lignin, cellulose and chitosan are being used for fluorescence and NIR imaging and photothermal therapy. But due to the uncertainty with the lignin based CQDs, it is less utilized for photodynamic therapy. Table 3 shows the bio-medical applications of lignin-based quantum dots in detail.

It can be understood from literature that if researchers can unveil the exact fluorescence behaviour of LGQDS, it will make incredible changes in the biomedical field. Fluorescence mechanism needs to be addressed thoroughly before the utilization of LQDs in biomedical filed. The bioimaging ability and the drug loading and releasing efficiency of LCQDs confirm the utility as a functional biomedical agent but still the uncertainty has to be erased. Thus, in near future, similar to other CQDs, lignin derived CQDS and GQDs will emerge as promising candidates in biomedical field. In most of the studies, the cytotoxicity of the LQDs is very less. Nevertheless, in certain reports LCQDs were showing cytotoxicity, which might be attributed to the presence of other molecules in the biomass that were not eliminated in the separation process. Hence the development of suitable methods for the separation of lignin from biomass is highly inevitable. If this can be done flawlessly, the exact fluorescence mechanism can be identified, uncertainty in the molecular break down can be eliminated and thus higher will be the demand for LCQD in biomedicine. Other applications of LCQDs are shown in Fig. 7 . In comparing the properties of QDs developed from conventional resources and lignin, it is clear that it is possible to make LCQDs with good properties and applications in lower cost( Table 4).

The biomedical applications of lignin based CQDs are still in its primitive stages. Even though there are studies related to photodynamic therapy and therapeutic applications of CQDs, very limited studies are available on lignin based CQDs, due to uncertainty in the origin of the fluorescence, lack of knowledge of exact structure and synthesis-property relations. The microwave synthesis is a comparatively easy, low cost and environmentally friendly method of synthesis but the fractionation followed hydrothermal synthesis is preferred in many cases due to the formation of small sized dots. Even though the origin of fluorescence of lignin is unclear, the aggregation induced conjugation effect is the most accepted explanation; but modifications can be expected in near future. Conjugated π-domains-based band gap emission and the surface effects are considered as the reason behind the fluorescence of LCQDs. Intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) can also be utilized for expressing the fluorescence and the sensing properties of LCQDs. More clarity is still needed to explain the fluorescence of LCQDs. The effect of precursor on the fluorescence properties has to be probed, which would help in tuning the properties to a great extent. The cytotoxicity studies confirm the biocompatibility of lignin-based fluorescence CQDs and are widely used for imaging applications. The limited studies available on the drug loading application and photodynamic therapy can be considered as a gap, which can be addressed in near future and will revolutionize biomedical field. If the uncertainties are cleared, fluorescence LCQDs will definitely pay way towards the development of advanced biomedical devices. The anti-bacterial effects of LCQDs will be an interesting topic for those who are working in the biomedical field. LCQDs, owing to its advanced fluorescence imaging and drug delivery applications will restructure the entire biomedical field. Hence in near future, the current biomedical devices might be replaced by lignin quantum dot-based diagnostic and therapeutic devices. Moreover, the wound healing property of LCQDs is remaining an unexplored area. The ability of LCQDs and functionalized LCQDs can be optimized for wound healing and tissue regeneration.

BDS Deeraj: Writing – original draft. Saritha Appukuttan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Jitha S Jayan: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Sajitha S Jayan: Writing – original draft, Methodology.
